content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
  
 
April 21, 2020 
 
 
Adenike Adeyeye 
Western States Energy Manager and Senior Analyst 
Union of Concerned Scientists 
500 12th Street, Suite 340 
Oakland, CA 94607 
 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
 
Our File No. A-20-027 
 
Dear Ms. Adeyeye: 
 
 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).1 Please note that we are only providing advice 
under the post-government employment provisions of the Act and not under any other post-
government employment laws, such as Public Contract Code Section 10411. Also, we are not a 
finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we 
provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts 
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. Finally, 
nothing in this letter should be construed as an evaluation of any conduct which may already have 
taken place, and any conclusions drawn apply only to prospective actions. (See Regulation 
18329(c)(4)(A).) 
  
QUESTIONS 
 
 
(1) Does the One-Year Ban prohibit you from appearing before or communicating with the 
California Public Utilities Commission (“CPUC”) or its officers or employees at public workshops 
regarding the implementation of “The 100 Percent Clean Energy Act of 2018” jointly held by the 
CPUC, the California Energy Commission, and the California Air Resources Board, given that your 
employment with the CPUC ended on January 17, 2020 and that you are now employed by the 
Union of Concerned Scientists (“UCS”) as its Western States Energy Manager and Senior Analyst? 
 
 
(2) Does the Permanent Ban prohibit you from working on the CPUC’s R.17-09-020 or 
R.19-11-009 rulemakings on behalf of UCS? 
 
 
 
 
 
 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-20-027 
Page No. 2 
 
 
CONCLUSIONS 
 
 
(1) Yes. The One-Year Ban prohibits you from appearing before or communicating with the 
CPUC or its officers or employees at the public workshops cohosted by CPUC on behalf of UCS 
because UCS would compensate you for such an appearance, the CPUC is your former state 
administrative agency, and such an appearance would be for the purpose of influencing 
administrative action.  
 
 
(2) Because the Permanent Ban does not apply to the making of rules or policies of general 
applicability, the Permanent Ban generally does not prohibit you from working on the CPUC’s 
R.17-09-020 or R.19-11-009 rulemakings on behalf of UCS. However, the one year ban still 
applies.2 We also caution that the Permanent Ban may prohibit you from working on any 
component proceeding of these rulemakings involving a specific party or parties, such as related 
orders and petitions applicable only to the petitioner or a specific party or parties, or a decision 
involving a procurement requirement applicable to the specific contractor.  Accordingly, you should 
seek additional advice regarding whether the Permanent Ban would prohibit you from working on a 
particular component proceeding of either rulemaking, if you participated in that proceeding while 
employed by the CPUC, and if that proceeding may affect or has affected the rights or claims of a 
specific party or parties. 
 
FACTS AS REQUESTED BY REQUESTER 
 
 
You were the Chief of Staff to Commissioner Guzman Aceves at the California Public 
Utilities Commission (“CPUC”) from December 2018 to January 17, 2020, as well as an advisor to 
the Commissioner from April 2018 to January 17, 2020. While at the CPUC, you managed 
members of the Commissioner’s staff, and you advised the Commissioner on energy and water 
matters, including resource adequacy. 
 
 
According to the CPUC’s website, the CPUC’s Resource Adequacy Program has two goals: 
to provide sufficient resources to the California Independent System Operator to ensure the safe and 
reliable operation of the electricity grid in real time; and to provide appropriate incentives for the 
siting and construction of new resources needed for reliability in the future. 
 
 
In October 2017, the CPUC opened its R.17-09-020 rulemaking. The Summary for the 
CPUC’s Order instituting R.17-09-020 provides that the rulemaking’s purpose is to “oversee the 
resource adequacy program, make any changes and refinements to the program, and establish local 
and flexible procurement obligations applicable to load-serving entities beginning with the 2019 
compliance year.” The CPUC’s Order instituting R.17-09-020 expressly provides that R.17-09-020 
is the successor to R.14-10-010, and that R.14-10-010 addressed the same topics over the previous 
three years. 
 
 
 
2 Notwithstanding the Permanent Ban, we note that the One-Year Ban applies to any administrative or 
legislative action including rulemaking proceedings. Thus, the one-year ban prohibits you from appearing before or 
communicating with the CPUC or its officers or employees in regard to the CPUC's R.17-09-020 and R.19-11-009 
rulemakings on behalf of UCS until January 17, 2021. 


File No. A-20-027 
Page No. 3 
 
 
 
To date, R.17-09-020 has involved numerous component proceedings related to resource 
adequacy, including 41 rulings, 23 decisions and pending decisions, and petitions for modification 
of a decision. You state that you did the following work associated with R.17-19-020 while at the 
CPUC: you reviewed party comments, took ex parte meetings with parties, and discussed resource 
adequacy issues (including confidential issues) with CPUC Energy Division staff. You also state 
that you advised Commissioner Aceves on the following issues related to R.17-09-020:  
 
• Adopting annual and local flexible capacity requirements applicable to electric load serving 
entities. 
 
• Adopting changes to the Resource Adequacy Program, including new requirements for the 
implementation of multi-year local procurements beginning with the 2020 compliance year. 
 
• Denying petitions for modification of previous decisions under R.17-09-020. 
 
• Affirming resource adequacy import rules (and the grant of a stay of this decision). 
 
• Establishing a schedule and process for determining the qualifying capacity value of hybrid 
resources pursuant to a request filed by parties to R.17-09-020. 
 
 
In November 2019, the CPUC opened R.19-11-009 rulemaking. The Summary for the 
CPUC’s Order instituting R.19-11-009 provides that the rulemaking’s purpose is to “oversee the 
Resource Adequacy program, consider any changes and refinements to the program, and establish 
forward Resource Adequacy procurement obligations applicable to load-serving entities beginning 
with the 2021 compliance year.” The CPUC’s Order instituting R.19-11-009 expressly provides that 
R.19-11-009 is the successor to R.17-09-020, and that R.17-09-020 addressed the same topics over 
the previous two years. You state that your involvement in R.19-11-009 while at the CPUC was 
limited to reviewing the Order instituting R.19-11-009, and briefly discussing the Order with 
Commissioner Aceves, before the CPUC voted to institute R.19-11-009. 
 
 
You are now employed by the Union of Concerned Scientists (“UCS”), a nonprofit 
organization, as its “Western States Energy Manager and Senior Analyst.” According to its website, 
the UCS’s mission is to “use rigorous, independent science to solve our planet’s most pressing 
problems.” The CPUC, California Energy Commission, and the California Air Resources Board 
will soon jointly provide public workshops to discuss the implementation of “The 100 Percent 
Clean Energy Act of 2018” (Stats. 2018, Ch. 312, SB 100 De León; hereafter “SB 100”), which 
requires all retail sales of electricity to end-use customers and electricity procured to serve state 
agencies to be supplied by renewable energy and zero-carbon resources by December 31, 2045. 
 
ANALYSIS 
 
 
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the One-Year Ban and the Permanent Ban. 
 
 
 


File No. A-20-027 
Page No. 4 
 
 
The One-Year Ban 
 
 
The “One-Year Ban” prohibits a former state employee from appearing before or 
communicating with his or her former state administrative agency or its officers or employees on 
behalf of any other person for compensation for the purpose of influencing any administrative or 
legislative action or any discretionary action involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or 
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. 
(Section 87406; Regulation 18746.1(b)(5)(C).) 
 
 
The One-Year Ban applies to, among others, a designated employee of a state administrative 
agency, “an employee of a state administrative agency who should be designated in the agency’s 
conflict-of-interest code,” and “any officer, employee, or consultant of a state administrative agency 
who holds a position that entails the making, or participation in the making, of decisions that may 
foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest.” (Section 87406(d)(1); Regulation 
18746.1(a)(4).) As Chief of Staff and an advisor to Commissioner Aceves at the CPUC, you held 
positions that entailed the making, and participation in the making, of decisions that may 
foreseeably have a material effect on a financial interest. Therefore, you are subject to the One-Year 
Ban. 
 
 
You have asked whether the One-Year Ban prohibits you from attending public workshops 
to discuss the implementation of SB 100 jointly held by the CPUC, the California Energy 
Commission, and the California Air Resources Board (the “SB 100 Workshops”). Regulation 
18746.1(b) prohibits a former state employee subject to the One-Year Ban from making an 
appearance or communication before his or her former state administrative agency or its officers or 
employees if all of the following apply: 
 
• The former state employee has permanently left state service. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(1).) 
 
• The appearance or communication3 is made within one year of the former state employee 
leaving state service. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(2).) 
 
• The former state employee is compensated or promised compensation for the appearance or 
communication. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3).) 
 
• The appearance or communication is made on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or 
representative of that person.4 (Regulation 18746.1(b)(4).) 
 
• The appearance or communication is made by the former state employee for the purpose of 
influencing a legislative or administrative action, or any discretionary act involving the 
 
3 Regulation 18746.2(a) provides that “an appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, 
conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding with in writing or by electronic transmission, attending a meeting, 
and delivering or sending any communication.” 
 
4 For purposes of the Act, Section 82047 defines “person” as “an individual, proprietorship, firm, partnership, 
joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, limited liability company, association, committee, and 
any other organization or group of persons acting in concert.” 


File No. A-20-027 
Page No. 5 
 
 
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale 
or purchase of goods or property.5 (Regulation 18746.1(b)(5)(C).) 
 
• The appearance or communication is made by the former state employee before a state 
administrative agency of the former state employee. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(6)(C).) 
 
 
Based on the facts presented, you permanently left state service on January 17, 2020. For 
purposes of this analysis, we assume the SB 100 Workshops will occur prior to January 20, 2021, 
while you are still subject to the One-Year Ban. Because you are a UCS employee, any appearance 
or communication made by you at a SB 100 Workshop would be on behalf UCS,6 and you would be 
compensated by UCS for an appearance or communication on its behalf. Such an appearance or 
communication would be for the purpose of influencing administrative action because the objective 
of the SB 100 Workshops is to discuss SB 100’s implementation. And such an appearance or 
communication would be before the CPUC, the state administrative agency you worked for and 
represented prior to leaving state service, because the CPUC is a cohost of the SB 100 Workshops. 
 
 
Thus, the One-Year Ban prohibits you from appearing before or communicating with the 
CPUC or its officers or employees at an SB 100 Workshop to discuss SB 100’s implementation on 
behalf of UCS. 
 
The Permanent Ban 
 
 
The “Permanent Ban” on “switching sides” prohibits a former “state administrative official” 
from working for compensation on judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceedings in which the 
official participated while working for the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) 
The Permanent Ban is a lifetime ban which applies to any formal or informal appearance or any oral 
or written communication – or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in representing 
any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or communication – made 
with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in which the official 
participated while he or she served as a state administrative official. (Ibid.) 
 
 
For purposes of the Permanent Ban, Section 87400(b) defines “state administrative official” 
as “every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state administrative agency who as part of 
his or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other 
than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity.” The facts presented indicate that you 
engaged in CPUC proceedings in more than a clerical, secretarial, or ministerial manner while 
employed by the CPUC as Chief of Staff and as an advisor to Commissioner Aceves. Therefore, 
you are subject to the Permanent Ban with respect to those proceedings. 
 
 
You have asked whether the Permanent Ban prohibits you from working on the CPUC’s 
R.17-09-020 or R.19-11-009 rulemakings now that you have left state service and are employed by 
UCS. The Permanent Ban applies if all the following criteria are met: 
 
5 Regulation 18746.2(a) provides that a “formal or informal appearance or oral or written communication is for 
the purpose of influencing if it is made for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, 
opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or proceeding.”  
 
6 Other than an “appearance as a member of the general public” as defined in Regulation 18704(d)(2). 


File No. A-20-027 
Page No. 6 
 
 
• The former state administrative official has permanently left state service. (Regulation 
18741.1(a)(1).)  
 
• The former official is compensated or promised compensation for the appearance or 
communication. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(2).) 
 
• The former official makes an appearance or communication, or aids, advises, counsels, or 
assists in representing another person in making an appearance or communication before any 
officer or employee of any state administrative agency for the purpose of influencing a 
proceeding. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(3).) 
 
• The proceeding includes any proceeding in which the former official participated personally and 
substantially by making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.7 
(Regulation 18741.1(a)(4).) 
 
• The proceeding is the same proceeding in which the official participated. (Regulation 
18741.1(a)(5).) 
 
 
As noted above, you permanently left state service on January 17, 2020. Because you are a 
UCS employee, any appearance or communication which you make or assist in making, other than 
an appearance as a member of the general public under Regulation 18704(d)(2), would be on behalf 
of and would be compensated by UCS. 
 
 
The Permanent Ban does not apply to the making of rules or policies of general 
applicability, such as a rulemaking proceeding involving rules of general applicability. (Beale 
Advice Letter, No. A-00-146.) Under Section 87400(c), the Permanent Ban applies to a “judicial, 
quasi-judicial, or other proceeding” which is defined as “any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest 
or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative 
agency.” Although the Permanent Ban does not apply to the making of rules or policies of general 
applicability, it does apply to proceedings that affect the rights or claims of a specific party or 
parties. (Lowry Advice Letter, No. I-08-053.) 
 
The purpose of the CPUC’s R.17-09-020 and 19-11-009 rulemakings is to oversee the 
Resource Adequacy Program, make changes and refinements to the Program, and establish local 
and flexible procurement obligations applicable to load-serving entities. The Permanent Ban would 
not apply to these rulemakings or their component proceedings that make rules or policies of 
general applicability. However, we cation that the Permanent Ban may apply to component 
proceedings that involve a specific party or parties, such as any petition and order that affects the 
rights or claims of the petitioner or a specific party or parties, as well as a  decision involving 
procurement requirements applicable to a particular contractor, if you participated in that 
proceeding while employed by the CPUC. 
 
7 Regulation 18741.1(a)(4) provides that a state administrative official who was a supervisor is deemed to have 
participated in any proceeding that was “pending before” the official’s agency and was under his or her supervisory 
authority.  


File No. A-20-027 
Page No. 7 
 
 
For purposes of the Act’s post-government employment provisions, “participated” means to 
have taken part personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written 
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of confidential 
information as an officer or employee. (Section 87400(d).) A state administrative official 
“participated personally and substantially” in a proceeding by making, participating in making, or 
influencing a governmental decision. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(4).) 
 
You advised Commissioner Aceves on the following issues related to R.17-09-020: 
adopting annual local and flexible capacity requirements applicable to load-serving entities; 
adopting changes to the Resource Adequacy program, including new requirements for the 
implementation of multi-year local procurements to being with the 2020 compliance year; denying 
petitions for modification of previous decisions under R.17-09-020; affirming resource adequacy 
import rules (and the grant of a stay of this decision); and establishing a schedule and process for 
determining the qualifying capacity value of hybrid resources pursuant to a request filed by parties 
to R.17-09-020. Your participation in R.19-11-009 was more limited. You state that your 
involvement in R.19-11-009 was confined to reviewing the Order instituting R.19-11-009, and 
briefly discussing the Order with Commissioner Aceves before the CPUC voted to adopt it.  
 
Based on the facts presented, it appears that at least some component proceedings of R.17-
09-020 may involve petitions or orders specific to a particular party or parties, or decisions 
regarding procurement requirements under the CPUC’s Resource Adequacy Program specific to a 
particular utility or other load serving entity. Each of these types of petitions, orders, and decisions 
must be assessed individually to determine if it affects the rights and claims of a specific party or 
parties. The facts presented, however, are insufficient to determine whether the Permanent Ban 
would apply to you with respect to any particular petition, order, or decision. Therefore, if a specific 
component proceeding of either of the CPUC’s R.17-09-020 or R.19-11-009 rulemakings may 
affect or has affected the rights or claims of a specific party or parties, and if you have participated 
in that proceeding personally and substantially while employed by the CPUC, you should seek 
additional advice regarding whether the Permanent Ban would prohibit you from working on that 
proceeding. 
 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Sincerely,  
 
 
Dave Bainbridge 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
General Counsel  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Matthew F. Christy 
 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
 
MFC:aja

(1) Does the One-Year Ban prohibit you from appearing before or communicating with the

R.19-11-009 rulemakings on behalf of UCS?
sections.conclusion:
(1) Yes. The One-Year Ban prohibits you from appearing before or communicating with the 
CPUC or its officers or employees at the public workshops cohosted by CPUC on behalf of UCS 
because UCS would compensate you for such an appearance, the CPUC is your former state

administrative action.

applicability, the Permanent Ban generally does not prohibit you from working on the CPUC’s 
R.17-09-020 or R.19-11-009 rulemakings on behalf of UCS. However, the one year ban still 
applies.2 We also caution that the Permanent Ban may prohibit you from working on any 
component proceeding of these rulemakings involving a specific party or parties, such as related 
orders and petitions applicable only to the petitioner or a specific party or parties, or a decision 
involving a procurement requirement applicable to the specific contractor.  Accordingly, you should 
seek additional advice regarding whether the Permanent Ban would prohibit you from working on a 
particular component proceeding of either rulemaking, if you participated in that proceeding while 
employed by the CPUC, and if that proceeding may affect or has affected the rights or claims of a 
specific party or parties.

sections.analysis:
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the One-Year Ban and the Permanent Ban.  
 
 
The One-Year Ban 
 
 
The “One-Year Ban” prohibits a former state employee from appearing before or 
communicating with his or her former state administrative agency or its officers or employees on 
behalf of any other person for compensation for the purpose of influencing any administrative or 
legislative action or any discretionary action involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or 
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. 
(Section 87406; Regulation 18746.1(b)(5)(C).) 
 
 
The One-Year Ban applies to, among others, a designated employee of a state administrative 
agency, “an employee of a state administrative agency who should be designated in the agency’s 
conflict-of-interest code,” and “any officer, employee, or consultant of a state administrative agency 
who holds a position that entails the making, or participation in the making, of decisions that may 
foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest.” (Section 87406(d)(1); Regulation 
18746.1(a)(4).) As Chief of Staff and an advisor to Commissioner Aceves at the CPUC, you held 
positions that entailed the making, and participation in the making, of decisions that may 
foreseeably have a material effect on a financial interest. Therefore, you are subject to the One-Year 
Ban. 
 
 
You have asked whether the One-Year Ban prohibits you from attending public workshops 
to discuss the implementation of SB 100 jointly held by the CPUC, the California Energy 
Commission, and the California Air Resources Board (the “SB 100 Workshops”). Regulation 
18746.1(b) prohibits a former state employee subject to the One-Year Ban from making an 
appearance or communication before his or her former state administrative agency or its officers or 
employees if all of the following apply: 
 
• The former state employee has permanently left state service. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(1).) 
 
• The appearance or communication3 is made within one year of the former state employee 
leaving state service. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(2).) 
 
• The former state employee is compensated or promised compensation for the appearance or 
communication. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3).) 
 
• The appearance or communication is made on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or 
representative of that person.4 (Regulation 18746.1(b)(4).) 
 
• The appearance or communication is made by the former state employee for the purpose of 
influencing a legislative or administrative action, or any discretionary act involving the 
 
3 Regulation 18746.2(a) provides that “an appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, 
conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding with in writing or by electronic transmission, attending a meeting, 
and delivering or sending any communication.” 
 
4 For purposes of the Act, Section 82047 defines “person” as “an individual, proprietorship, firm, partnership, 
joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, limited liability company, association, committee, and 
any other organization or group of persons acting in concert.”  
 
 
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale 
or purchase of goods or property.5 (Regulation 18746.1(b)(5)(C).) 
 
• The appearance or communication is made by the former state employee before a state 
administrative agency of the former state employee. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(6)(C).) 
 
 
Based on the facts presented, you permanently left state service on January 17, 2020. For 
purposes of this analysis, we assume the SB 100 Workshops will occur prior to January 20, 2021, 
while you are still subject to the One-Year Ban. Because you are a UCS employee, any appearance 
or communication made by you at a SB 100 Workshop would be on behalf UCS,6 and you would be 
compensated by UCS for an appearance or communication on its behalf. Such an appearance or 
communication would be for the purpose of influencing administrative action because the objective 
of the SB 100 Workshops is to discuss SB 100’s implementation. And such an appearance or 
communication would be before the CPUC, the state administrative agency you worked for and 
represented prior to leaving state service, because the CPUC is a cohost of the SB 100 Workshops. 
 
 
Thus, the One-Year Ban prohibits you from appearing before or communicating with the 
CPUC or its officers or employees at an SB 100 Workshop to discuss SB 100’s implementation on 
behalf of UCS. 
 
The Permanent Ban 
 
 
The “Permanent Ban” on “switching sides” prohibits a former “state administrative official” 
from working for compensation on judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceedings in which the 
official participated while working for the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) 
The Permanent Ban is a lifetime ban which applies to any formal or informal appearance or any oral 
or written communication – or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in representing 
any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or communication – made 
with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in which the official 
participated while he or she served as a state administrative official. (Ibid.) 
 
 
For purposes of the Permanent Ban, Section 87400(b) defines “state administrative official” 
as “every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state administrative agency who as part of 
his or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other 
than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity.” The facts presented indicate that you

employed by the CPUC as Chief of Staff and as an advisor to Commissioner Aceves. Therefore, 
you are subject to the Permanent Ban with respect to those proceedings. 
 
 
You have asked whether the Permanent Ban prohibits you from working on the CPUC’s 
R.17-09-020 or R.19-11-009 rulemakings now that you have left state service and are employed by 
UCS. The Permanent Ban applies if all the following criteria are met: 
 
5 Regulation 18746.2(a) provides that a “formal or informal appearance or oral or written communication is for 
the purpose of influencing if it is made for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, 
opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or proceeding.”  
 
6 Other than an “appearance as a member of the general public” as defined in Regulation 18704(d)(2).  
 
 
• The former state administrative official has permanently left state service. (Regulation 
18741.1(a)(1).)  
 
• The former official is compensated or promised compensation for the appearance or 
communication. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(2).) 
 
• The former official makes an appearance or communication, or aids, advises, counsels, or 
assists in representing another person in making an appearance or communication before any 
officer or employee of any state administrative agency for the purpose of influencing a 
proceeding. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(3).) 
 
• The proceeding includes any proceeding in which the former official participated personally and 
substantially by making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.7 
(Regulation 18741.1(a)(4).) 
 
• The proceeding is the same proceeding in which the official participated. (Regulation 
18741.1(a)(5).) 
 
 
As noted above, you permanently left state service on January 17, 2020. Because you are a 
UCS employee, any appearance or communication which you make or assist in making, other than 
an appearance as a member of the general public under Regulation 18704(d)(2), would be on behalf 
of and would be compensated by UCS. 
 
 
The Permanent Ban does not apply to the making of rules or policies of general 
applicability, such as a rulemaking proceeding involving rules of general applicability. (Beale 
Advice Letter, No. A-00-146.) Under Section 87400(c), the Permanent Ban applies to a “judicial, 
quasi-judicial, or other proceeding” which is defined as “any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest 
or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative 
agency.” Although the Permanent Ban does not apply to the making of rules or policies of general 
applicability, it does apply to proceedings that affect the rights or claims of a specific party or 
parties. (Lowry Advice Letter, No. I-08-053.) 
 
The purpose of the CPUC’s R.17-09-020 and 19-11-009 rulemakings is to oversee the 
Resource Adequacy Program, make changes and refinements to the Program, and establish local 
and flexible procurement obligations applicable to load-serving entities. The Permanent Ban would 
not apply to these rulemakings or their component proceedings that make rules or policies of 
general applicability. However, we cation that the Permanent Ban may apply to component 
proceedings that involve a specific party or parties, such as any petition and order that affects the 
rights or claims of the petitioner or a specific party or parties, as well as a  decision involving 
procurement requirements applicable to a particular contractor, if you participated in that 
proceeding while employed by the CPUC. 
 
7 Regulation 18741.1(a)(4) provides that a state administrative official who was a supervisor is deemed to have 
participated in any proceeding that was “pending before” the official’s agency and was under his or her supervisory 
authority.   
 
 
For purposes of the Act’s post-government employment provisions, “participated” means to 
have taken part personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written 
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of confidential 
information as an officer or employee. (Section 87400(d).) A state administrative official 
“participated personally and substantially” in a proceeding by making, participating in making, or 
influencing a governmental decision. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(4).) 
 
You advised Commissioner Aceves on the following issues related to R.17-09-020: 
adopting annual local and flexible capacity requirements applicable to load-serving entities; 
adopting changes to the Resource Adequacy program, including new requirements for the 
implementation of multi-year local procurements to being with the 2020 compliance year; denying 
petitions for modification of previous decisions under R.17-09-020; affirming resource adequacy 
import rules (and the grant of a stay of this decision); and establishing a schedule and process for 
determining the qualifying capacity value of hybrid resources pursuant to a request filed by parties 
to R.17-09-020. Your participation in R.19-11-009 was more limited. You state that your 
involvement in R.19-11-009 was confined to reviewing the Order instituting R.19-11-009, and 
briefly discussing the Order with Commissioner Aceves before the CPUC voted to adopt it.  
 
Based on the facts presented, it appears that at least some component proceedings of R.17-
09-020 may involve petitions or orders specific to a particular party or parties, or decisions 
regarding procurement requirements under the CPUC’s Resource Adequacy Program specific to a 
particular utility or other load serving entity. Each of these types of petitions, orders, and decisions 
must be assessed individually to determine if it affects the rights and claims of a specific party or 
parties. The facts presented, however, are insufficient to determine whether the Permanent Ban 
would apply to you with respect to any particular petition, order, or decision. Therefore, if a specific 
component proceeding of either of the CPUC’s R.17-09-020 or R.19-11-009 rulemakings may 
affect or has affected the rights or claims of a specific party or parties, and if you have participated 
in that proceeding personally and substantially while employed by the CPUC, you should seek 
additional advice regarding whether the Permanent Ban would prohibit you from working on that 
proceeding.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: (1) Does the One-Year Ban prohibit you from appearing before or communicating with the 
California Public Utilities Commission (“CPUC”) or its officers or employees at public workshops 
regarding the implementation of “The 100 Percent Clean Energy Act of 2018” jointly held by the 
CPUC, the California Energy Commission, and the California Air Resources Board, given that your 
employment with the CPUC ended on January 17, 2020 and that you are now employed by the 
Union of Concerned Scientists (“UCS”) as its Western States Energy Manager and Senior Analyst? 
 
 
(2) Does the Permanent Ban prohibit you from working on the CPUC’s R.17-09-020 or 
R.19-11-009 rulemakings on behalf of UCS?

CONCLUSION: (1) Yes. The One-Year Ban prohibits you from appearing before or communicating with the 
CPUC or its officers or employees at the public workshops cohosted by CPUC on behalf of UCS 
because UCS would compensate you for such an appearance, the CPUC is your former state 
administrative agency, and such an appearance would be for the purpose of influencing 
administrative action.  
 
 
(2) Because the Permanent Ban does not apply to the making of rules or policies of general 
applicability, the Permanent Ban generally does not prohibit you from working on the CPUC’s 
R.17-09-020 or R.19-11-009 rulemakings on behalf of UCS. However, the one year ban still 
applies.2 We also caution that the Permanent Ban may prohibit you from working on any 
component proceeding of these rulemakings involving a specific party or parties, such as related 
orders and petitions applicable only to the petitioner or a specific party or parties, or a decision 
involving a procurement requirement applicable to the specific contractor.  Accordingly, you should 
seek additional advice regarding whether the Permanent Ban would prohibit you from working on a 
particular component proceeding of either rulemaking, if you participated in that proceeding while 
employed by the CPUC, and if that proceeding may affect or has affected the rights or claims of a 
specific party or parties. 
 
FACTS AS REQUESTED BY REQUESTER 
 
 
You were the Chief of Staff to Commissioner Guzman Aceves at the California Public 
Utilities Commission (“CPUC”) from December 2018 to January 17, 2020, as well as an advisor to 
the Commissioner from April 2018 to January 17, 2020. While at the CPUC, you managed 
members of the Commissioner’s staff, and you advised the Commissioner on energy and water 
matters, including resource adequacy. 
 
 
According to the CPUC’s website, the CPUC’s Resource Adequacy Program has two goals: 
to provide sufficient resources to the California Independent System Operator to ensure the safe and 
reliable operation of the electricity grid in real time; and to provide appropriate incentives for the 
siting and construction of new resources needed for reliability in the future. 
 
 
In October 2017, the CPUC opened its R.17-09-020 rulemaking. The Summary for the 
CPUC’s Order instituting R.17-09-020 provides that the rulemaking’s purpose is to “oversee the 
resource adequacy program, make any changes and refinements to the program, and establish local 
and flexible procurement obligations applicable to load-serving entities beginning with the 2019 
compliance year.” The CPUC’s Order instituting R.17-09-020 expressly provides that R.17-09-020 
is the successor to R.14-10-010, and that R.14-10-010 addressed the same topics over the previous 
three years. 
 
 
 
2 Notwithstanding the Permanent Ban, we note that the One-Year Ban applies to any administrative or 
legislative action including rulemaking proceedings. Thus, the one-year ban prohibits you from appearing before or 
communicating with the CPUC or its officers or employees in regard to the CPUC's R.17-09-020 and R.19-11-009 
rulemakings on behalf of UCS until January 17, 2021.  
 
 
 
To date, R.17-09-020 has involved numerous component proceedings related to resource 
adequacy, including 41 rulings, 23 decisions and pending decisions, and petitions for modification 
of a decision. You state that you did the following work associated with R.17-19-020 while at the 
CPUC: you reviewed party comments, took ex parte meetings with parties, and discussed resource 
adequacy issues (including confidential issues) with CPUC Energy Division staff. You also state 
that you advised Commissioner Aceves on the following issues related to R.17-09-020:  
 
• Adopting annual and local flexible capacity requirements applicable to electric load serving 
entities. 
 
• Adopting changes to the Resource Adequacy Program, including new requirements for the 
implementation of multi-year local procurements beginning with the 2020 compliance year. 
 
• Denying petitions for modification of previous decisions under R.17-09-020. 
 
• Affirming resource adequacy import rules (and the grant of a stay of this decision). 
 
• Establishing a schedule and process for determining the qualifying capacity value of hybrid 
resources pursuant to a request filed by parties to R.17-09-020. 
 
 
In November 2019, the CPUC opened R.19-11-009 rulemaking. The Summary for the 
CPUC’s Order instituting R.19-11-009 provides that the rulemaking’s purpose is to “oversee the 
Resource Adequacy program, consider any changes and refinements to the program, and establish 
forward Resource Adequacy procurement obligations applicable to load-serving entities beginning 
with the 2021 compliance year.” The CPUC’s Order instituting R.19-11-009 expressly provides that 
R.19-11-009 is the successor to R.17-09-020, and that R.17-09-020 addressed the same topics over 
the previous two years. You state that your involvement in R.19-11-009 while at the CPUC was 
limited to reviewing the Order instituting R.19-11-009, and briefly discussing the Order with 
Commissioner Aceves, before the CPUC voted to institute R.19-11-009. 
 
 
You are now employed by the Union of Concerned Scientists (“UCS”), a nonprofit 
organization, as its “Western States Energy Manager and Senior Analyst.” According to its website, 
the UCS’s mission is to “use rigorous, independent science to solve our planet’s most pressing 
problems.” The CPUC, California Energy Commission, and the California Air Resources Board 
will soon jointly provide public workshops to discuss the implementation of “The 100 Percent 
Clean Energy Act of 2018” (Stats. 2018, Ch. 312, SB 100 De León; hereafter “SB 100”), which 
requires all retail sales of electricity to end-use customers and electricity procured to serve state 
agencies to be supplied by renewable energy and zero-carbon resources by December 31, 2045.

Also, we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. Finally, nothing in this letter should be construed as an evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place, and any conclusions drawn apply only to prospective actions. (See Regulation 18329(c)(4)(A).) (2) Does the Permanent Ban prohibit you from working on the CPUC’s R.17-09-020 or R.19-11-009 rulemakings on behalf of UCS? 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-20-027 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS (1) Yes. The One-Year Ban prohibits you from appearing before or communicating with the CPUC or its officers or employees at the public workshops cohosted by CPUC on behalf of UCS because UCS would compensate you for such an appearance, the CPUC is your former state administrative agency, and such an appearance would be for the purpose of influencing administrative action. (2) Because the Permanent Ban does not apply to the making of rules or policies of general applicability, the Permanent Ban generally does not prohibit you from working on the CPUC’s R.17-09-020 or R.19-11-009 rulemakings on behalf of UCS.
